figures.label: Figure 3.0-2.
figures.caption: Each stage is crucial to the successful implementation of a new technology. Negative results at any stage may halt the implementation of the SUS. An example of such a negative decision will be a case in which there will be an adverse effect on patient safety or where there is no feasible solution to a technical problem. The aim of the gated approach is to fail the process quickly when a major problem cannot be solved. The decision pathway is not necessarily linear—the end user may decide to enter the pathway at any given point. However, each stage must be addressed to have a balanced decision process prior to the implementation of an SUS.

figures.label: Figure 5.11.2-1
figures.caption: in which a bioprocess container (BC) was investigated for leaks. Individual reports for each leak were analyzed and the location of each leak was mapped and identified according to type and cause (57).

figures.label: Figure 5.11.2-2
figures.caption: ). The map indicated the leaks were more likely to occur at the bottom and top of the bag (i.e., where the BC was handled by operators). Subsequently, the operators were trained to avoid damage due to mishandling, and this led to a significant decline in leak rates (57).

figures.label: Table 3.1.4-1
figures.caption: offers a summary of common positive controls or engineered defects. Engineered defects for positive controls can have the added advantage of being a micron-sized defect, but other factors should also be considered: • Percentage of the micron-sized defects that can be consistently and reliably detected using a cho- sen CCIT method (NOTE: All positive controls with leaks at or above the claimed limit of detection (LoD) of the test method must be detected with high confidence (e.g., 95%). • Size and geometry of leaks that occur in real life and the ability of the test method to accurately detect those defects. • Leak size needed for microbial contamination to occur and pose a risk to product sterility or qual- ity over the entire lifecycle of the product.

figures.label: Fig. 4.1.1-1
figures.caption: ). Other interfaces are the needle–needle shield interface and the barrel–needle shield interface. For more detailed information on syringe design see PDA Technical Report No. 73: Prefilled Syringe User Requirements for Biotechnology Applications (7).

figures.label: Figure 4.1.1-2
figures.caption: highlights the key interfaces for staked needles.

figures.label: Figure 4.1.1-1
figures.caption: Diagram of Prefilled Syringe

figures.label: Fig. 4.1.1-2
figures.caption: ). The barrel–closure interface comprises the tip of the syringe and the corresponding sealing surface of the closure. Careful consideration should be given to closure design and elastomeric formulation because different closure systems respond differently to changes in environmental condi- tions (e.g., heat, sterilization cycles, or CCIT method cycles). Proper interface fit between the closure system and syringe barrel is an essential requirement to ensure CCI.

figures.label: Figure D.1.
figures.caption: As air is being sampled at each designated sampling location, the running total particle count is continuously compared to the expected count for the proportion of the prescribed total volume that has been sampled. If the running total count is less than the lower limit Cpass corresponding to the expected count, the air being sampled is found to meet the specified class or concentration limit, and sampling is halted.
